title: Flash Attention on Apple Silicon
description: Why naïve attention dies at long context, how Flash Attention's tiling rewrites it to fit in fast on-chip memory, and what's specific to MPS — kernel paths in PyTorch, MLX's flash attention, and the limits of porting CUDA-style flash to Metal.

sections.title: Why Naïve Attention Hits a Wall
sections.content:
Stock self-attention has a memory problem. For a sequence of length T with head dim d_k, the **score matrix** Q @ K^T is T × T — and the **softmax weights** matrix is the same size again. Both are materialized in fast memory (GPU HBM on CUDA, unified memory on MPS), then a third T × T tensor is allocated to hold attention weights, then they're matmul'd against V. Do the math. For Llama-3-8B with 32 heads, head_dim = 128, batch = 1, sequence T = 8192: - Score matrix: `B · n_heads · T · T · sizeof(fp16)` = `1 · 32 · 8192 · 8192 · 2 bytes` = **4.3 GB**. - Softmax probabilities: another 4.3 GB. - Total intermediate just for attention at one layer: ~**8.5 GB**. With 32 layers, the peak intermediate footprint is way past anything that fits in an M3 Max's unified memory. Long-context attention is **memory-bound, not compute-bound** — and on Apple Silicon, where you don't have CUDA's enormous HBM bandwidth, the pain shows up sooner. ```mermaid flowchart LR Q["Q (T × d_k)"] K["K (T × d_k)"] V["V (T × d_k)"] Q --> S["Scores = Q K^T
(T × T)
materialised"] K --> S S --> A["softmax
(T × T)
materialised"] A --> O["O = A V
(T × d_k)"] V --> O classDef big fill:#7a1d2f,stroke:#f85149,color:#fff class S,A big ``` The two red boxes are the killers. Anything you can do to avoid materializing them wins.

sections.title: The Flash Attention Idea — Tiled Online Softmax
sections.content:
Dao et al. (2022) noticed that the entire T × T attention matrix doesn't need to exist at once. You only need it to compute the output row-by-row — and you can do that with **online softmax**, which folds the sum and max running as you stream over K and V tiles.

The rewrite, in words:

1. Split Q, K, V into tiles along the sequence dimension. A Q tile is small (say 64 rows); a K/V tile likewise.
2. For each Q tile, loop over all K/V tile pairs. Compute the partial scores Q_block · K_block^T, take softmax incrementally, multiply by V_block, accumulate into the output for that Q tile.
3. Track per-row running statistics — the running max `m_i` and running denominator `l_i` — so you can correct previous partial outputs as you encounter new tiles with larger scores.

The online softmax recurrence:

```
m_new = max(m_old, max(scores))
rescale = exp(m_old - m_new)
l_new = l_old · rescale + sum(exp(scores - m_new))
O_new = (O_old · l_old · rescale + exp(scores - m_new) · V_block) / l_new
```

Every quantity is small (fits in a thread block's shared memory / threadgroup memory). The full T × T matrix is never allocated.

**Memory drops from O(T²) to O(T · d_k).** The numerics are bit-identical to standard attention (modulo floating-point reduction order). Throughput on long contexts goes up 3-5x because you're no longer round-tripping intermediates through slow HBM.

sections.title: Where Flash Attention Lives in PyTorch
sections.content:
On CUDA, `torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention()` (SDPA) automatically routes to one of several backends, with Flash Attention 2/3 as the preferred path for long sequences. The dispatcher picks based on:

- Sequence length, head dim, dtype, mask shape
- Available kernels for your GPU architecture
- Whether `attn_mask` is one the kernel supports

On **MPS**, the same SDPA call dispatches to MPS-specific kernels. As of PyTorch 2.5+ those kernels include a Flash-Attention-style tiled implementation written in Metal Shading Language. The exact backend selection is an Apple/Meta moving target — early MPS attention was naïve and slow; recent releases ship genuine FA kernels for common shapes.

To use Flash Attention on MPS today, the recipe is:

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

B, H, T, D = 1, 32, 8192, 128
device = 'mps' if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else 'cpu'
q = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=torch.float16)
k = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=torch.float16)
v = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=torch.float16)

# This is the only call you need — SDPA picks the best kernel for you.
out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
```

Three things to check:

1. **dtype is fp16 or bf16, not fp32.** The flash MPS kernel is gated on half precision; fp32 falls back to the naïve path.
2. **Mask is `is_causal=True` or `None`.** Arbitrary masks fall back to the slow path.
3. **Sequence is long enough.** Below ~512 tokens the tiling overhead loses to the simpler kernel; SDPA's dispatcher knows this.

Verify which kernel ran with `torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(...)` on CUDA; on MPS there's no equivalent flag yet (Apple controls the dispatch internally), so the only way to confirm Flash is being used is to measure memory: peak MPS memory should be ~O(T·d_k) per layer, not O(T²).

sections.title: MLX's Flash Attention
sections.content:
Apple's own MLX framework ships a Flash Attention primitive separately from PyTorch's MPS backend. Same algorithm, written natively for Metal, integrated with MLX's lazy graph compilation.

```python
import mlx.core as mx
from mlx_lm.models.base import scaled_dot_product_attention

B, H, T, D = 1, 32, 8192, 128
q = mx.random.normal(shape=(B, H, T, D), dtype=mx.float16)
k = mx.random.normal(shape=(B, H, T, D), dtype=mx.float16)
v = mx.random.normal(shape=(B, H, T, D), dtype=mx.float16)

out = mx.fast.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, scale=1.0 / (D ** 0.5))
mx.eval(out)  # MLX is lazy — force evaluation here
```

`mx.fast.scaled_dot_product_attention` is the documented entry point. It dispatches to a tiled Metal kernel. Performance on M-series silicon for long-context Llama inference is competitive with PyTorch MPS in 2026; for some shapes MLX wins by 10-30% because of its more aggressive kernel fusion.

The **practical reason** to choose one over the other:

- **PyTorch on MPS** — when your model code already exists in PyTorch and you don't want to rewrite. Most fine-tuning workflows.
- **MLX** — when you control the model architecture and want best Apple-Silicon-native performance. mlx-lm's pre-trained Llama/Mistral/Qwen variants typically outperform their PyTorch-on-MPS counterparts on tokens/sec by 15-40%.

sections.title: What's Different on Metal vs CUDA
sections.content:
Flash Attention's algorithm is portable — the math doesn't care what hardware it runs on. But the kernel implementation does, and Metal differs from CUDA in ways that matter:

**Threadgroup memory is the new shared memory.** CUDA's per-block shared memory is ~48-100 KB; Metal's threadgroup memory is similar (typically 32 KB on M-series, varies). The Flash tile sizes (Q_BLOCK_M = 64 or 128, K_BLOCK_N = 64) are chosen to fit one Q tile + one K/V pair + accumulators + softmax stats in threadgroup memory. That budget is roughly the same on Metal as on Ampere/Hopper.

**SIMD groups, not warps.** Apple GPUs execute threads in **simdgroups** of 32 (sometimes 16 on older architectures). The CUDA-style warp-shuffle reductions used in Flash 2's softmax become `simd_shuffle` / `simd_max` / `simd_sum` in Metal Shading Language. Functionally equivalent; the kernel writer changes intrinsic names.

**No Tensor Cores.** The biggest gap. Flash Attention 3 on H100 gets enormous speedup from `wgmma` (warp-group MMA) calling Hopper Tensor Cores. Apple Silicon has no equivalent matrix-multiply hardware in the GPU; matmuls run on regular SIMD ALUs. The peak FLOPs you can extract per byte of memory traffic is therefore lower on MPS — Flash Attention's memory wins help even more (because compute isn't your competitive advantage), but you'll never match an H100 on raw throughput.

**Unified memory is a free lunch (sometimes).** On CUDA, every byte the kernel touches has been DMA'd from system RAM into HBM. On Apple Silicon, HBM and RAM are the same physical pool — `to('mps')` is essentially a relabel, not a copy. For attention specifically this means you can stream over a multi-GB KV cache that lives in unified memory without paying PCIe transfer costs. Long-context inference benefits disproportionately.

**Kernel launch overhead is higher.** Metal's command-buffer model has higher per-launch overhead than CUDA. For Flash Attention this isn't usually the bottleneck (you launch one kernel per attention layer), but if you're benchmarking small workloads you'll see it.

### Performance ballpark

Llama-3-8B inference at T=4096, decode phase, Q4 quantized:

| Backend | tok/s on M3 Max |
|---|---|
| Naïve attention, fp16 | 18 |
| PyTorch MPS + Flash kernel | 35 |
| MLX + Flash + fp16 | 42 |
| MLX + Flash + INT4 KV | 52 |

For reference: an RTX 4090 with vLLM does about 95 tok/s on the same model. The gap is dtype/kernel maturity, not the algorithm.

sections.title: Pitfalls and Practical Recipes
sections.content:
Four things that bite people running Flash Attention on Apple Silicon.

### 1. Half-precision dtype is mandatory

The Flash kernels in both PyTorch-MPS and MLX are dtype-gated to fp16 and bf16. Pass fp32 inputs and you silently get the slow path. **Cast Q/K/V explicitly:** `q, k, v = (t.half() for t in (q, k, v))`. Outputs come back in the same dtype.

### 2. KV cache layout matters

For autoregressive decoding, the K and V tensors grow by one row per generated token. If you reallocate them every step, you spend more time on `torch.cat` than on attention. **Pre-allocate to max sequence length** and write into a slice:

```python
# Wrong (slow):
k_cache = torch.cat([k_cache, new_k], dim=2)

# Right (fast):
k_cache[:, :, t, :] = new_k
# attention reads k_cache[:, :, :t+1, :]
```

The Flash kernel handles the variable visible-length correctly when passed via `is_causal=True`.

### 3. Masks beyond causal force the fallback

Attention masks come in two flavors: structural (causal, sliding window) and content (padding, prefix). Causal is fast. **Custom masks tank you back to the naïve path.** If you absolutely need a custom mask, either:
- Pad your sequences to the same length so causal alone suffices, OR
- Apply the mask to your input embeddings (multiply attention scores externally to the SDPA call), accepting that you're back to O(T²) memory.

### 4. Profile, don't trust

The MPS attention dispatcher changes between PyTorch releases. A workload that hit the fast kernel in 2.4 might fall back in 2.5 if your shape lands outside the new gating. **Measure peak memory and per-step latency on actual inputs**, not synthetic benchmarks. Use `torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory()` and a simple wall clock.

```python
import torch, time
torch.mps.synchronize()
start_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory()
t0 = time.perf_counter()
out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
torch.mps.synchronize()
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
peak_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory() - start_mem
print(f'{elapsed*1000:.1f} ms, peak mem +{peak_mem / 1024**2:.1f} MiB')
```

If `peak_mem` is anywhere near `B * n_heads * T * T * 2` bytes, you're on the slow path. Fix one of the above and re-measure.

### What to take away

1. Flash Attention's win is memory, not magic — every modern attention kernel uses it.
2. On Apple Silicon you reach it through `F.scaled_dot_product_attention()` (PyTorch) or `mx.fast.scaled_dot_product_attention()` (MLX). Don't write your own.
3. The kernel is gated on fp16/bf16 + causal/no mask. Step outside those constraints and you're back to O(T²).
4. MLX consistently edges out PyTorch-on-MPS for new model code; PyTorch-MPS wins for ecosystem inertia.
5. There's still no Tensor Core equivalent on Apple GPUs — but Flash Attention is the optimization where that matters least, because it's bandwidth-bound.

quiz.question: For a Llama-3-8B attention layer at T=8192 with fp16, what is the approximate memory footprint of the materialized score + softmax matrices, and why does Flash Attention avoid it?
options: ~100 MB; Flash avoids it by running on the CPU instead of GPU.
~8.5 GB total (2 × T·T matrices × n_heads × 2 bytes); Flash avoids materializing them by tiling and using online softmax to compute output rows incrementally.
~500 MB; Flash uses gradient checkpointing.
~1 GB; Flash quantizes to INT8.
quiz.explanation: Naïve attention materializes a T×T score matrix and a T×T softmax matrix per head — at T=8192 with 32 heads in fp16 that's 32·8192·8192·2 = 4.3 GB each, ~8.5 GB total per layer. Flash tiles Q over K/V, computes per-tile partial outputs, and uses the online softmax recurrence (m, l, O updates) to merge them without ever holding the full T×T matrix in memory. Memory drops from O(T²) to O(T·d_k).

quiz.question: You call `F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)` on MPS with fp32 inputs. Why does memory still scale as O(T²)?
options: Causal masking is broken on MPS.
MPS doesn't support attention at all.
PyTorch's MPS Flash kernel is gated on half precision (fp16/bf16); fp32 silently falls back to the naïve O(T²) implementation.
scaled_dot_product_attention always allocates the full score matrix.
quiz.explanation: Flash kernels — on both CUDA and MPS — are dtype-gated. fp32 takes a different (slower) backend path. Cast Q/K/V to fp16 or bf16 to land on the Flash kernel. There's no error; only a memory and time regression to notice.

quiz.question: What's the role of the online softmax recurrence in Flash Attention?
options: Make softmax differentiable.
Allow softmax to be computed incrementally over tiles by tracking running max and denominator, so previous partial outputs can be rescaled when a new tile contains larger scores.
Replace softmax with a cheaper function.
Avoid numerical underflow in fp16.
quiz.explanation: Naïve softmax needs the global max and global sum of exp(scores). Online softmax tracks both as running statistics (m, l). When a new tile arrives with a larger max, you rescale the previously accumulated output by exp(m_old - m_new) before adding the new tile's contribution. The final output matches standard softmax bit-for-bit (modulo FP reduction order), but no T×T matrix exists at any point.

quiz.question: The same Flash Attention algorithm runs on both Hopper (H100) and Apple M3 silicon. Why is the H100 still 2-3× faster on Llama inference even when both use Flash?
options: Apple GPUs have less memory.
H100 has Tensor Cores (wgmma) that execute the per-tile matmuls at much higher density than Apple's SIMD ALUs, which lack matrix-multiply hardware in the GPU.
H100 uses CUDA, which is faster than Metal as a language.
MPS doesn't actually use Flash Attention.
quiz.explanation: The per-tile attention math is dominated by Q@K^T and softmax@V matmuls. On Hopper, those land on wgmma instructions that drive Tensor Cores at ~6× the throughput of regular FP16 ALUs. Apple's GPU has no Tensor Core equivalent — matmuls run on standard SIMD ALU. Flash Attention closes most of the memory-bandwidth gap, but the compute-density gap remains.

quiz.question: Why does pre-allocating a fixed-size KV cache and writing into slices outperform `torch.cat` during autoregressive decoding?
options: `cat` is a buggy operation on MPS.
`cat` reallocates the underlying buffer every step, copying every existing K/V byte each token. Pre-allocating once and writing into a slice avoids the per-step O(T) memcpy.
MPS doesn't support `cat`.
It only matters for fp32.
quiz.explanation: Each `torch.cat` call allocates a new (T+1)-row buffer and copies the old T rows in. After N decode steps that's O(N²) total copy work. Pre-allocating to max_seq_len and writing into `k_cache[:, :, t, :]` keeps every step at O(d_k) work for the new row only. Production LLM inference (vLLM, mlx-lm, TGI) all do this; on MPS the win is the same shape as on CUDA.

quiz.question: Which of these will reliably trigger the SLOW (non-Flash) attention path on MPS?
options: Sequence length 4096, fp16, is_causal=True.
Sequence length 4096, bf16, is_causal=True, batch size 8.
Sequence length 4096, fp16, a hand-crafted boolean attn_mask of shape (T, T) other than causal.
Sequence length 4096, fp16, no mask, contiguous tensors.
quiz.explanation: The Flash MPS kernel handles is_causal=True or no mask cheaply because both have a regular structure. A general (T, T) boolean mask forces the dispatcher to fall back to the naïve path that can apply arbitrary masks. If you must use a non-causal mask, consider whether you can apply it externally to a Flash result, or restructure your problem (e.g., pad to causal).

quiz.question: How do you verify on MPS that your attention call actually hit the Flash kernel and not the fallback?
options: Set torch.backends.mps.flash_only = True; it errors if Flash isn't available.
Print `attention.__name__`; it says 'flash' on the fast path.
Measure peak MPS memory with `torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory()` before and after; if peak growth is O(T²) you're on the slow path, if it's O(T·d_k) you got Flash.
There's no way to tell.
quiz.explanation: Unlike CUDA's `sdp_kernel(...)` context manager, MPS doesn't expose a kernel-selection flag yet. The reliable signal is memory: the slow path materializes T×T matrices (gigabytes at long context), Flash doesn't. Wrap your call in driver_allocated_memory measurements and check the delta against B·n_heads·T·T·sizeof(dtype). Order-of-magnitude off → you're on Flash.

exercise.title: Measure Flash vs naïve attention on MPS
exercise.description:
Build the smallest possible benchmark that proves Flash Attention is doing what it claims on your machine.

**Setup.** PyTorch ≥ 2.5 with MPS available. The script auto-falls-back to CPU if MPS is missing.

**Part A — Wall-time scan.** For each `T ∈ [512, 1024, 2048, 4096]`, measure `F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)` once. Use B=1, n_heads=32, head_dim=128, dtype=fp16. Print T, time (ms), tok/sec (T/elapsed).

**Part B — Peak-memory check.** Wrap the call in `driver_allocated_memory()` reads and report peak growth. Compute the naïve expected memory (`B · n_heads · T · T · sizeof(fp16)`) for comparison. If your peak is < 10% of the naïve estimate → you're on Flash. If it's ~100% → you're on the slow path.

**Part C — Force the slow path.** Repeat Part A but pass an explicit `attn_mask` (a non-causal boolean of shape (T, T)). Compare wall time and memory to Part A. The mask should not change the output materially (use upper-triangular = -inf so it's effectively causal) but it WILL force the slow kernel. Report the ratio.

Expected: Part A scales sub-quadratically in time and is roughly linear in memory. Part C scales O(T²) in both.

Runs locally; ~30 seconds total wall time on an M-series Mac.
exercise.starterCode:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import time

device = 'mps' if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else 'cpu'
print(f'device = {device}')
if device != 'mps':
    print('  (this exercise is meaningful on MPS — CPU numbers will be different)')

B, H, D = 1, 32, 128
DTYPE = torch.float16

def make_qkv(T):
    q = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    k = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    v = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    return q, k, v

def time_attn(T, mask=None):
    q, k, v = make_qkv(T)
    # Warmup
    for _ in range(2):
        F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, attn_mask=mask, is_causal=(mask is None))
    if device == 'mps':
        torch.mps.synchronize()
        start_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, attn_mask=mask, is_causal=(mask is None))
    if device == 'mps':
        torch.mps.synchronize()
        peak_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory() - start_mem
    else:
        peak_mem = 0
    elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
    return elapsed, peak_mem

# Part A: time + memory for is_causal=True
print('\nPart A — Flash path (is_causal=True):')
print(f"{'T':>5} {'ms':>9} {'peak MiB':>10} {'naive MiB':>11} {'on-flash?':>10}")
for T in [512, 1024, 2048, 4096]:
    elapsed, peak = time_attn(T)
    naive = B * H * T * T * 2 / 1024**2
    on_flash = 'yes' if peak / 1024**2 < naive * 0.1 else 'NO'
    print(f'{T:>5} {elapsed:>9.1f} {peak/1024**2:>10.1f} {naive:>11.1f} {on_flash:>10}')

# Part C: force the slow path by passing an explicit mask. TODO: implement.
print('\nPart C — Slow path (explicit causal-ish mask):')
print('TODO: build a (T, T) bool mask of upper-triangular -inf and pass it as attn_mask=')
exercise.solution:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import time

device = 'mps' if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else 'cpu'
print(f'device = {device}')

B, H, D = 1, 32, 128
DTYPE = torch.float16

def make_qkv(T):
    q = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    k = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    v = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device=device, dtype=DTYPE)
    return q, k, v

def time_attn(T, mask=None, is_causal=True):
    q, k, v = make_qkv(T)
    for _ in range(2):
        F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, attn_mask=mask, is_causal=is_causal)
    if device == 'mps':
        torch.mps.synchronize()
        start_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, attn_mask=mask, is_causal=is_causal)
    if device == 'mps':
        torch.mps.synchronize()
        peak_mem = torch.mps.driver_allocated_memory() - start_mem
    else:
        peak_mem = 0
    elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
    return elapsed, peak_mem

print('\nPart A — Flash path (is_causal=True):')
print(f"{'T':>5} {'ms':>9} {'peak MiB':>10} {'naive MiB':>11} {'on-flash?':>10}")
for T in [512, 1024, 2048, 4096]:
    elapsed, peak = time_attn(T)
    naive = B * H * T * T * 2 / 1024**2
    on_flash = 'yes' if peak / 1024**2 < naive * 0.1 else 'NO'
    print(f'{T:>5} {elapsed:>9.1f} {peak/1024**2:>10.1f} {naive:>11.1f} {on_flash:>10}')

print('\nPart C — Slow path (explicit causal-ish bool mask, is_causal=False):')
print(f"{'T':>5} {'ms':>9} {'peak MiB':>10} {'naive MiB':>11} {'slow?':>8}")
for T in [512, 1024, 2048]:
    # An upper-triangular boolean mask is effectively causal but forces SDPA's
    # dispatcher off the fast path. (True = masked-out, per PyTorch convention.)
    mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, dtype=torch.bool, device=device), diagonal=1)
    elapsed, peak = time_attn(T, mask=mask, is_causal=False)
    naive = B * H * T * T * 2 / 1024**2
    slow = 'yes' if peak / 1024**2 > naive * 0.5 else 'NO?'
    print(f'{T:>5} {elapsed:>9.1f} {peak/1024**2:>10.1f} {naive:>11.1f} {slow:>8}')

# On an M3 Max with PyTorch 2.5:
#   Part A scales sub-quadratically; peak mem ~T·d_k.
#   Part C scales quadratically; peak mem matches the naive estimate within ~10%.
# If your numbers DON'T show this gap, either:
#   - You're on a PyTorch version without the MPS Flash kernel — upgrade.
#   - The dispatcher gate has moved; check Apple's PRs against pytorch/pytorch.
